name: metabolomics-workbench-database
description: Access NIH Metabolomics Workbench via REST API (4,200+ studies). Query metabolites, RefMet nomenclature, MS/NMR data, m/z searches, study metadata, for metabolomics and biomarker discovery.
capabilities: Query metabolite structures by PubChem CID, InChI Key, HMDB ID, KEGG ID
Search metabolomics studies by metabolite, disease, species, and analytical method
Standardize metabolite names using RefMet classification hierarchy
Perform mass spectrometry m/z searches with ion adduct support
Access gene and protein information from MGP database
Download molecular structures as MOL or PNG files
author: K-Dense-AI
securitySummary: All 344 static findings are FALSE POSITIVES. The analyzer misidentified markdown documentation code blocks as shell commands, mass spectrometry ion adduct notation (M+H, M-H) as weak cryptographic algorithms, and legitimate database API calls as suspicious network activity. This is a documentation-only skill for the NIH Metabolomics Workbench - a legitimate scientific research database. No executable code exists.
valueStatement: Search and retrieve metabolomics data from the NIH Metabolomics Workbench database containing 4,200+ studies. Access metabolite structures, study metadata, RefMet nomenclature, and perform mass spectrometry searches.
limitations: Does not provide wet-lab experimental protocols
No metabolite pathway analysis or visualization tools
Cannot perform statistical analysis on study data
API rate limits apply for high-volume queries

useCases.title: Find studies for a metabolite
useCases.description: Search all metabolomics studies containing measurements of a specific metabolite like glucose or citrate.

useCases.title: Identify compounds from MS data
useCases.description: Input m/z values from mass spectrometry to identify potential metabolite candidates matching your data.

useCases.title: Filter studies by disease
useCases.description: Find metabolomics studies for specific diseases like diabetes or cancer using specific analytical platforms.

promptTemplates.title: Find metabolite studies
promptTemplates.prompt: Find all studies in the Metabolomics Workbench that contain measurements of [metabolite_name]. Return study IDs and brief descriptions.

promptTemplates.title: Identify compound from m/z
promptTemplates.prompt: Search the Metabolomics Workbench for compounds matching m/z [value] with [M+H] adduct and 0.5 Da tolerance. List the top matches.

promptTemplates.title: Standardize metabolite name
promptTemplates.prompt: Use RefMet to standardize the metabolite name [common_name]. Return the standardized name and classification hierarchy.

promptTemplates.title: Filter by disease and platform
promptTemplates.prompt: Find human blood metabolomics studies on [disease_name] using [LCMS or GCMS or NMR] analytical method.

bestPractices: Use RefMet to standardize metabolite names before searching studies for consistent results
Set appropriate mass tolerance based on your instrument resolution (0.5 Da for low-res, 0.01 Da for high-res MS)
Cache Ref making many queries toMet database locally when reduce API calls
antiPatterns: Searching for metabolites without using RefMet standardization may miss relevant studies
Using excessive mass tolerance returns too many false positive matches
Making rapid sequential requests without delays may trigger rate limiting

faq.question: What is the Metabolomics Workbench?
faq.answer: NIH Common Fund-sponsored repository at UCSD hosting 4,200+ metabolomics studies with standardized data.

faq.question: What is RefMet?
faq.answer: Standardized metabolite nomenclature database with hierarchical classification from broad class to specific compound.

faq.question: What ion adducts should I use?
faq.answer: M+H for positive mode ESI, M-H for negative mode ESI. Use M+Na if sodium adducts are common in your samples.

faq.question: How do I download metabolite structures?
faq.answer: Use the compound context with PNG output format to download structure images, or MOL for molecular format files.

faq.question: What study parameters can I filter by?
faq.answer: Filter by analysis type, polarity, chromatography, species, sample source, disease, and specific metabolites via MetStat.

faq.question: Does this skill require API authentication?
faq.answer: No, the NIH Metabolomics Workbench REST API is freely accessible without authentication for public data.
